title: Les directions
description: Asking for and giving directions: prepositions, direction verbs, common landmarks.

cards.back: to the right / on the right
cards.notes: Always with **à**. *Tournez à droite* — turn right. The opposite is *à gauche*.

cards.back: to the left / on the left

cards.notes: Two words. Note the spelling — *tout* (straight) without the **e**, *droit* without the **e** either. Both invariable in this fixed phrase.
Followed by **de** + noun: *près de la gare* (near the station). *De* contracts with *le* / *les*: **près du** marché, **près des** boutiques.
Same construction as *près de*: **loin du** centre, **loin des** magasins.
*La poste est en face de la gare* — the post office is across from the station.
Irregular: *je vais*, *tu vas*, *il/elle va*, *nous allons*, *vous allez*, *ils/elles vont*. Used for both location movement and the near-future construction (*aller* + infinitive = going to).
Irregular: *je prends*, *tu prends*, *il prend*, *nous prenons*, *vous prenez*, *ils prennent*. *Prenez la première rue à droite* — take the first street on the right.
Starts with a vowel — elision. Wider than a *rue*, usually with trees down the middle in classic city design.
Specifically for trains. Buses use *la gare routière* or just *la station*.
**Le poste** (m.) is something else entirely — a TV / radio set, or a job position. Gender disambiguates.

steps.title: Asking for directions
steps.body:
# Directions in French

Getting lost in a French-speaking country is normal — and asking for help is a great way to practice. The basic ask:

- *Excusez-moi, où est **la gare**, s'il vous plaît ?* — Excuse me, where is the station, please?
- *Excusez-moi, je cherche **le musée**.* — Excuse me, I'm looking for the museum.

This lesson covers what comes BACK in the answer: location prepositions (right, left, near, opposite), direction verbs (go, turn, take), and common landmarks. By the end you'll be able to follow a basic spoken direction.

steps.title: Location prepositions
steps.body:
## Where things are

| French | English | Example |
|---|---|---|
| à droite (de) | to the right (of) | *à droite de la poste* |
| à gauche (de) | to the left (of) | *à gauche de la rue* |
| tout droit | straight ahead | *Continuez tout droit.* |
| près de | near | *près de la gare* |
| loin de | far from | *loin du centre* |
| en face de | opposite | *en face de l'hôtel* |
| à côté de | next to | *à côté du café* |

The four with **de** at the end contract with the article: **de + le → du**, **de + les → des**.

- *près **du** parc* (parc is m.) — near the park
- *à côté **des** boutiques* — next to the shops
- *en face **de la** poste* (la, no contraction) — opposite the post office

exercise.prompt: Match each French location word with its English meaning.
*La poste est à droite de la gare.* What does this mean?

images.label: The post office is to the right of the station.
The post office is to the left of the station.
The post office is far from the station.
The post office is next to the station.

exercise.hint: *à droite de* — to the right of.
distractors: The post office is to the left of the station.
The post office is far from the station.
The post office is next to the station.

steps.title: Direction verbs
steps.body:
## Verbs for getting around

Three key verbs:

**aller** (to go) — irregular
- *Vous **allez** tout droit.* — Go straight.
- *Je **vais** à la gare.* — I'm going to the station.

**tourner** (to turn) — regular -er
- *Tournez à droite.* — Turn right. (formal/plural imperative)
- *Tourne à gauche.* — Turn left. (informal imperative)

**prendre** (to take) — irregular
- *Prenez la première rue à droite.* — Take the first street on the right.
- *Je prends le bus.* — I'm taking the bus.

Directions are usually given in the **vous** imperative — strangers asking strangers. The verb drops the subject pronoun: *Tournez* (not *Vous tournez*).

exercise.hint: Imperative *vous* form: drop the pronoun, keep the *-ez* ending.

steps.title: Build: go straight then turn left

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *Go straight, then turn left.*

steps.title: Asking — phrases to memorise
steps.body:
## Stock asking phrases

These four cover most situations:

- ***Excusez-moi*** — Excuse me (always start with this when stopping a stranger)
- ***Où est ___ ?*** — Where is ___?
- ***Comment aller à ___ ?*** — How do I get to ___? (lit. *how to go to*)
- ***Je cherche ___*** — I'm looking for ___

Full example:

- *Excusez-moi, où est la poste, s'il vous plaît ?* — Excuse me, where is the post office, please?

French listeners will appreciate the *Excusez-moi* opener — it's the politeness signal that you respect them stopping for you. Skip it and you sound rude even with the rest of the sentence intact.

exercise.prompt: Match each French phrase with its English meaning.

pairs.right: I'm looking for the post office.

pairs.left: Comment aller à la rue Victor Hugo ?
pairs.right: How do I get to Victor Hugo street?

exercise.prompt: Politely ask where the train station is. (Translate: *Excuse me, where is the station, please?*)
accept: Excusez-moi, où est la gare, s'il vous plaît ?
Excusez-moi, où est la gare, s'il vous plaît?
Excusez-moi, où est la gare, s'il vous plaît.
exercise.hint: Three parts: politeness opener + the question + polite ending. French puts a space before *?* — but most input methods accept either way.

exercise.prompt: Which sentence means *Take the first street on the right*?

images.label: Prenez la première rue à droite.
Tournez la première rue à droite.
Allez la première rue à droite.
Prenez la première rue à gauche.

distractors: Tournez la première rue à droite.
Allez la première rue à droite.
Prenez la première rue à gauche.

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *The station is near the post office.*
exercise.hint: Subject + *est* + preposition phrase. No contraction needed (*de la*, not *du*).
